The correlation suggests cohesion and coupling are jointly determined by the gateway pattern — zones behind gateways achieve both simultaneously, while zones without gateways fail both metrics. This co-determination is not documented in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md but is a testable architectural invariant.

The monorepo's architectural enforcement has a temporal asymmetry: policy tests (architecture-policy.test.js, domain-isolation.test.js) catch violations at CI time, but zone insights and findings are only updated when sourcevision analysis is explicitly re-run. A finding that appears in sourcevision zone insights but is not yet encoded as a policy test exists in a 'detected but unenforced' state that can persist indefinitely — the gap between detection (sourcevision) and enforcement (policy tests) is the primary reason violations accumulate rather than being resolved promptly after discovery.

Cross-zone edge labels use abbreviated names ('fix', 'rex-unit') that diverge from canonical zone IDs ('rex-fix-command', 'rex-prd-engine'). This is a systemic data consistency gap: any analysis tool that joins cross-zone edge records with zone metadata using zone ID as the join key will silently drop rows for these two zones, producing undercount coupling metrics and missing bidirectional cycle detection for the affected zones.

The web-viewer → panel edge (4 imports) means the parent zone depends on one of its own satellite sub-zones, creating a downward containment dependency. Panel cannot be extracted from web-viewer without breaking 4 web-viewer callers, yet panel's architectural purpose is to be a bounded sub-zone of web-viewer. This mutual containment makes zone boundary evolution structurally harder than any other bidirectional edge in the codebase because extraction requires simultaneous changes on both sides.

web-unit (test zone) imports 7 symbols from web-viewer and 1 from web-shared — making it the most production-coupled test zone by absolute import count. The 7 web-viewer imports significantly exceed the single violation in the opposite direction (web-viewer→web-unit: 1 import), suggesting web-unit contains substantial test helper infrastructure tightly coupled to viewer internals rather than testing through the public API surface.

The two lowest-cohesion source zones in the monorepo (web: 0.55, crash: 0.5) both participate in bidirectional import cycles with web-viewer. Across all zones, participation in a bidirectional cycle is the strongest predictor of low cohesion — suggesting bidirectional coupling is a causal driver of cohesion degradation, not merely a co-occurring symptom. Breaking a single direction of each cycle would measurably improve cohesion scores on the next analysis pass without any structural refactor.

The monorepo enforces architectural rules at three distinct tiers: (1) npm package boundaries enforced by Node.js module resolution (strongest), (2) gateway files validated by domain-isolation.test.js (medium), and (3) zone conventions verified by sourcevision analysis passes (weakest). Every confirmed architectural violation exists in zones governed only by tier 3. Promoting any one web-package internal boundary to tier 2 by adding a boundary-check.test.ts assertion would immediately raise its enforcement strength without requiring any production code changes.

The src/landing/ directory is the only directory in the web package where community detection splits a single feature's files across two zones (web-application-core for static assets, web-landing-page for source and tests). This is not a community-detection artifact — it reflects a genuine import-edge gap between HTML/CSS assets (no edges) and TypeScript source (import edges) causing the algorithm to assign them to different nearest-neighbor clusters.

Static and binary assets (PNG, HTML, CSS) and build scripts (build.js, dev.js) have no import edges and are assigned to whichever zone is geographically closest in the directory tree, inflating file counts and deflating true cohesion scores for web-viewer-messaging-pipeline and web-application-core. Excluding non-TS/JS files from zone assignment would yield more accurate cohesion metrics for both zones without any structural change to the source code.

The web package has two files whose names imply one concern but whose content addresses another: data-loading-efficiency.test.ts (name implies latency, content is pagination API contract) and shared-types.ts (name implies low-risk type aliases, content is a stability-marked cross-zone interface contract with a conformance test suite). Naming drift of this kind is a maintenance risk because contributors make refactoring-scope decisions based on file names before reading content.

Across all five web zones, the only zone-level barrels that exist are crash/index.ts and web-shared/index.ts — the three larger zones (web-application-core, web-viewer-messaging-pipeline, web-task-usage-scheduler) have no zone-level barrel file. This creates an asymmetric discoverability pattern: small satellite zones have explicit public API entry points while the largest zone relies entirely on import-graph analysis to determine its boundaries.

The components/index.ts barrel exists and is comprehensive for value exports but is incomplete for type exports (missing ActiveRun, ActiveTasksPanelProps) — a barrel that exports components but not their prop/data types forces consumers to mix barrel and direct imports, defeating single-entry-point enforcement. The pattern of exporting only the component function while omitting its associated interface types is a systematic gap across the components barrel.

hench-runs.ts bypasses the components barrel for all six of its panel imports despite the barrel existing — this suggests the barrel was introduced after hench-runs.ts was written and the existing imports were never migrated. The barrel has zero practical consumers for its panel exports, making it aspirational infrastructure rather than an enforced contract.

The architectural fix for the crash ↔ viewer structural zone cycle (change crash-detector.ts line 16 from '../external.js' to '../shared/view-id.js') remains unimplemented despite being identified, documented in module comments, and having a known one-line fix. The absence of a boundary-check.test.ts assertion blocking the external.ts import path is the only reason this has not been caught automatically.

The integration-coverage-policy proportional ratio (15% of E2E file count) currently resolves to the same value as MIN_INTEGRATION_FILES (5), making the ratio clause a no-op at current suite sizes — the ratio will not exert independent pressure on integration test growth until the E2E suite exceeds 33 files (ceil(34×0.15)=6>5); consider raising MIN_INTEGRATION_FILES to 6 to restore independent enforcement

gateway-pipeline.test.js and hench-rex-gateway-pipeline.test.js use inconsistent naming conventions within the same integration-tests zone — one is scoped to its consumer/upstream pair, the other is not; a consistent '{consumer}-{upstream}-gateway-pipeline.test.js' naming convention across all integration files would make the coverage map readable without opening each file

The two non-test files in the e2e-tests zone (e2e-helpers.js and verify-build.js) follow no naming convention that distinguishes them from test files at a glance in directory listings; a subdirectory (e.g. tests/e2e/helpers/) or an explicit naming convention (e2e-util-*.js) would make zone membership boundaries visible without relying on sourcevision analysis

The REQUIRED_CONTRACTS package-name matching strategy in integration-coverage-policy.test.js will produce false-passing results for any future package that contributes two or more gateways (as hench currently does) — the pattern should be considered a known fragility to address before the next gateway is added to hench or web

Establish a policy that every critical or warning finding produced by sourcevision zone enrichment must be accompanied by a corresponding policy-test assertion within the same PR that resolves the finding. The current workflow (analyze → insight → eventual fix) allows findings to age indefinitely; a 'findings must be enforced or explicitly waived' policy would create a mechanical link between detection and resolution.

The dual-gateway pattern demonstrated by hench (one gateway file per upstream package, partitioned by tier) should replace the current single-gateway documentation in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md. All three are governed only by zone conventions (enforcement tier 3). Adding a boundary-check.test.ts assertion for each zone's import direction would promote them to enforcement tier 2 and yield the highest risk reduction per unit of effort of any single architectural improvement available.
